summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that Fickling's incomplete module blocklist fails to detect ctypes.CDLL as overtly malicious. The malicious pickle payload successfully loads the native C library when unpickled, but Fickling only classifies it as SUSPICIOUS (severity 2/5) instead of LIKELY_OVERTLY_MALICIOUS (severity 4/5).
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should demonstrate that a malicious pickle using ctypes.CDLL('libc.so.6') evades Fickling's detection (classified as SUSPICIOUS) and successfully loads the native C library when unpickled, establishing a native code execution pathway.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched the target Fickling library, created a malicious pickle payload using ctypes.CDLL('libc.so.6'), verified that Fickling detects it as only SUSPICIOUS (not OVERTLY_MALICIOUS), and confirmed successful loading of the native library interface.
verification_method: The exploit script creates a malicious pickle payload using ctypes.CDLL, runs Fickling's check_safety() on it, verifies the severity is below LIKELY_OVERTLY_MALICIOUS (bypass successful), then unpickles the payload and verifies it successfully loads the native C library (exploit successful).
evidence_produced_by_run: Fickling check_safety() returns Severity.SUSPICIOUS (level 2/5) for the ctypes.CDLL('libc.so.6') payload
Payload successfully unpickles to a ctypes CDLL object, confirming native library access
The detection gap allows native code execution vectors to evade proper classification

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the Fickling repository from GitHub on the host, checks out the main branch, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
